user_query:
I’m a contemporary artist, and my uncle—who helps fund my studio—prefers very traditional work. I need to make a tactful case for a few exhibitions that blend our tastes. I saved three gallery webpage snapshots under input/webpages/. Please build a small, local-only scraper that reads those HTML files, filters for exhibitions that could appeal to both of us, and produces structured outputs I can share.

Requirements:
1) Update config/scrape_config.yaml to set these preferences (modify the existing file, don’t create a new path):
   - filters.include_styles: ["Abstract", "Mixed Media", "New Media", "Contemporary"]
   - filters.exclude_styles: ["Hyperrealism", "Neoclassical"]
   - filters.allowed_medium_keywords: ["oil", "printmaking", "sculpture", "mixed media"]
   - filters.date_range.start: "2024-11-01"
   - filters.date_range.end: "2025-12-31"
   Leave input_glob and output paths as-is.

2) Implement a local scraper (you may write a script) that:
   - Reads HTML files at input/webpages/*.html (do not fetch anything from the Internet).
   - For each <div class="exhibition"> on a page, extract:
     • gallery_name: from the page’s <h1>
     • exhibition_title: from <h2>
     • start_date and end_date: split from <span class="dates"> formatted as "YYYY-MM-DD to YYYY-MM-DD"
     • mediums: from <span class="mediums"> split by semicolons
     • styles: from <span class="styles"> split by commas
   - Apply filters (case-insensitive): include an exhibition only if
     • it has at least one style in filters.include_styles,
     • it has no style in filters.exclude_styles,
     • at least one filters.allowed_medium_keyword is a substring of any medium, and
     • start_date >= filters.date_range.start AND end_date <= filters.date_range.end.
   - Write the results sorted by start_date ascending to both outputs:
     • CSV at output/exhibitions.csv with header: gallery_name,exhibition_title,start_date,end_date,mediums,styles (mediums must be semicolon-separated; styles comma-separated).
     • JSON at output/exhibitions.json as a list of objects with the same fields.

3) Draft and rewrite two messages for my uncle using the scraped results (make these files plain text):
   - output/email_candid.txt: a candid, 180–250 word message that acknowledges our stylistic disagreements, explains why these shows bridge our tastes, and lists up to three matching exhibitions using lines formatted as “Gallery — Exhibition (YYYY-MM-DD to YYYY-MM-DD) — key mediums”. Use the exact gallery and exhibition titles from the CSV.
   - output/email_diplomatic.txt: a warmer, respectful, concise rewrite (120–180 words) of the same message that keeps the facts and exhibition list but softens disagreements and focuses on common ground.

Deliverables:
- Modified config/scrape_config.yaml with the preferences above.
- output/exhibitions.csv and output/exhibitions.json matching each other and the filters.
- output/email_candid.txt and output/email_diplomatic.txt that accurately reflect the data in the CSV/JSON.

Please do not hardcode specific results; derive them from the provided HTML snapshots and the updated config. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
